petitioner_background: The petitioner is a physician specializing in gastroenterology, specifically in advanced procedures such as endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) and ERCP. He worked as a house staff physician at Coney Island Hospital in Brooklyn from June 2002 through June 2005, and subsequently as a physician at Maimonides Medical Center, which is affiliated with the Mount Sinai School of Medicine in New York. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences pursuant to section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel initially asserted the petitioner was submitting evidence of 'Awards and Memberships in Societies,' but under this heading only discussed the petitioner's alleged 'leading and critical roles,' his work as a 'prolific teacher,' the publication of the petitioner's case studies, his presentations and his memberships. No actual evidence of prizes or awards was submitted.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO concurred with the director that no evidence was submitted of lesser nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards. All of the evidence discussed by counsel under this heading related to other criteria and none of those claimed accomplishments constitute prizes or awards.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted letters from the American Society for Gastrointestinal Endoscopy (ASGE) and the New York Society for Gastrointestinal Endoscopy (NYSGE) affirming his membership. He also submitted evidence that ASGE has over 9,000 members 'who utilize endoscopy as a diagnostic and therapeutic method of treatment for diseases of the digestive tract.' The petitioner submitted evidence that the American College of Gastroenterology is open to those who meet certain education requirements. Counsel asserted the petitioner had been 'selected for membership to the most respected and renowned gastroenterology societies in the country and the world.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The ASGE materials did not indicate or suggest that ASGE requires outstanding achievements of its members; rather, it appears to be a professional association open to most if not all members of the profession. The petitioner did not submit the membership requirements for the NYSGE, which appears to be a local association. Regarding the American College of Gastroenterology, the petitioner did not demonstrate he is a member, and regardless, meeting education requirements is not an outstanding achievement — the education required appears commensurate with the petitioner's occupation, and it does not appear that recognized national or international experts judge prospective members. The AAO concurred with the director that the societies of which the petitioner is a member do not require outstanding achievements.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner relied primarily on reference letters. A letter from a governor of the American College of Gastroenterology (who was also an assistant professor at Mount Sinai School of Medicine and had coauthored a study with the petitioner presented at the 2006 ACG meeting) asserted the petitioner is 'one of our nation's foremost experts' in endoscopic ultrasound (EUS), noting that Maimonides Medical Center is one of only a few centers equipped with this modality. A letter from the Director of the Division of Liver Diseases at Mount Sinai School of Medicine asserted the petitioner's case studies 'have the potential to lead to improvements in the course of treatment for gastroenterology patients.' A letter from a physician at Mount Sinai (who had coauthored studies with the petitioner) discussed the importance and complexity of colorectal screening. A letter from an associate professor at Indiana University School of Medicine (also an ACG governor, invited speaker at Mount Sinai in 2006) asserted there is a shortage of qualified gastroenterologists and that the petitioner's expertise in hepatology and advanced therapeutic ERCP allows him to manage cirrhosis, end stage liver disease, and Hepatitis, and that the petitioner uses 'the latest medications' and took part in a liver study that 'provided critical insight.' A letter from an assistant professor at the University of Arkansas (who did his residency at Our Lady of Mercy Medical Center in New York) praised the petitioner's expertise with modern procedures and discussed a single patient treated by the petitioner. The petitioner also submitted published and presented case studies in distinguished journals and conferences, plus an exhibit entitled 'citations' consisting of Scholar.google.com searches for his last name and Yahoo.com searches for his coauthor's name.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that expert opinions cannot form the cornerstone of a successful claim, and that letters containing vague claims are less persuasive than those identifying specific contributions and examples of influence. Letters from references previously aware of the petitioner through reputation are more persuasive than those merely reviewing his CV. Several letter writers had personal associations with the petitioner (coauthorships, colleagues at Mount Sinai) rather than independent knowledge through his reputation. The AAO found that the letters failed to explain how learning to use technology developed by others or passing that knowledge to interns is an original contribution. One letter provided no examples of independent hospitals adopting new techniques or case studies. Another did not explain how using technology developed by others is an original contribution or provide examples of how the petitioner's ability to perform procedures developed by others has impacted the field. The shortage of qualified gastroenterologists is a labor issue under Department of Labor jurisdiction per NYSDOT, 22 I&N Dec. at 221, not relevant to extraordinary ability. Merely having a diverse skill set is not an original contribution of major significance. Letters did not provide examples of unique techniques developed by the petitioner being adopted nationwide, and prescribing medications developed by others is neither original nor of major significance. Regarding case studies, the Chest journal materials indicated it publishes 'cutting edge clinical investigations' in addition to 'case studies,' suggesting not every case study represents a cutting edge investigation. The submitted 'citations' exhibit did not establish any citations — Scholar.google.com links did not list any citations, and the Yahoo.com search for a coauthor's name was unexplained. The AAO concluded that without letters from independent hospitals or clinics adopting his procedures, evidence of wide and frequent citation, or comparable evidence of impact, he cannot meet this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Although counsel did not explicitly assert the petitioner met this criterion, he submitted evidence of published and presented case studies in journals and at conferences. Several references asserted that invitations to present his work at different locations demonstrated widespread notoriety.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that the record lacks any evidence that other clinicians have cited or otherwise relied on his case studies. The AAO stated that even if it were to conclude the petitioner meets this criterion because publication is not necessarily inherent to the occupation of a physician, the petitioner falls far short of meeting any other criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner indicated on his curriculum vitae that he has worked as a physician and house staff physician for hospitals in New York City, including Coney Island Hospital and Maimonides Medical Center (affiliated with Mount Sinai School of Medicine). Counsel asserted the petitioner performed 'leading and critical roles.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: While the petitioner may have performed a 'leading role' on a particular case study, the criterion requires looking at his role for a specific organization or establishment. The position must be of such significance that the alien's selection to fill the position, in and of itself, is indicative of or consistent with national or international acclaim. The AAO was not persuaded that either the physician or house staff physician position is leading or critical beyond the obvious need for a hospital to employ competent physicians.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, concurring with the director that the petitioner had not established the sustained national or international acclaim necessary for classification as an alien of extraordinary ability. Counsel's sole assertion on appeal — that the director erred because the RFE only asked for evidence of intent to continue in the field — was rejected; the AAO noted that 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(8) allows denial based on evidence of ineligibility notwithstanding lack of required initial evidence, and that neither counsel nor the petitioner attempted to address the deficiencies identified in the denial. On the final merits determination, the AAO found that even in the aggregate, the evidence did not distinguish the petitioner as one of the small percentage who has risen to the very top of the field; his reliance on professional memberships, case study publications, and colleague accolades fell short, especially when compared to reference letter writers themselves who held significantly more prominent positions (e.g., Director of Medical Education and Research, Vice President of a society, authorship of 13 books and book chapters, editorial board positions). The top of the petitioner's field appeared to be far higher than the level he had attained.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791, 795 (Commr. 1988)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Regl. Commr. 1972)
Matter of New York State Dep't of Transp., 22 I&N Dec. 215, 221 (Commr. 1998)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Counsel's sole argument on appeal was a procedural one: that the director erred in denying the petition because the RFE only asked for evidence of the petitioner's intent to continue in his field. The AAO rejected this, noting that under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(8), a petition shall be denied based on evidence of ineligibility notwithstanding any lack of required initial evidence. The AAO acknowledged the RFE could have been more comprehensive but found that approving a petition lacking supporting evidence is not an appropriate remedy, and that neither counsel nor the petitioner attempted to address the deficiencies identified in the denial or identify evidence that might have been submitted. Additionally, the petitioner's submitted 'citations' exhibit was misleading — it consisted of Scholar.google.com searches that did not actually show any citations, and an unexplained Yahoo.com search for a coauthor's last name.
